parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: Someone made a really loud, offhand joke about how I never react to anything anymore. It used to just roll right off my shoulders, but today it just sat there. I tried to laugh it off like I normally would, but the words just felt completely hollow. I kept staring at the screen and wondering why my usual responses feel so heavy now. It’s just a weird feeling to watch a familiar moment happen and feel absolutely nothing behind it.
turns.model_response:
That sounds like a really disorienting and lonely experience. Sometimes the things we’ve learned to brush off can suddenly feel heavy when we’re already carrying something else inside—even if we’re not fully aware of what that “something else” is.

It’s okay if your usual defenses don’t work the same way they used to. That shift might be your mind or heart telling you that something needs attention—maybe exhaustion, maybe a change in how you see yourself or others, maybe just needing a moment to acknowledge that you’re not in the same place you were before.

You don’t have to force the laugh or the lightness if it’s not there. It’s okay to sit with the quiet, even if it feels strange. Sometimes the most honest thing we can do is just notice how we feel without trying to fix it right away.

If you want to talk more about what’s been on your mind lately—or even if you just want to sit with the feeling—I’m here.

turns.user_message: That same kind of comment keeps popping up lately, and it’s starting to wear me down. I used to brush it off without a second thought, but now it just highlights how empty everything feels. I catch myself going through the same daily routines, waiting for a spark that never actually shows up. Even when good things happen around me, it’s like there’s a thick glass wall between me and the moment. I just keep nodding along while everything else fades into the background.
turns.model_response:
That sounds incredibly heavy to carry, and I’m really sorry you’re feeling this way. It’s exhausting when things that used to bounce off start to stick, and when the routines that once felt normal begin to feel hollow. That sense of watching life happen from behind a glass wall—like you’re there but not really *there*—is a lonely and draining place to be.

It’s okay to acknowledge that you’re worn down. Sometimes, when we’ve been pushing through for a long time, our emotional reserves just run low. The fact that you’re noticing this shift—that you’re aware of the emptiness creeping in—means you’re still in touch with yourself, even if it doesn’t feel that way right now.

Would you feel comfortable sharing a little more about what these comments are, or what they seem to stir up in you? Sometimes naming the thing that’s weighing on us can help take a little of its power away. But if you’d rather not, that’s okay too. Either way, I’m here to listen.

turns.user_message: I keep hearing those same remarks echo in my head, and it’s exhausting trying to pretend I’m still the same person. The things that used to bring me joy now just feel like chores I have to complete. I sit through entire days watching everything happen around me while my own reactions stay completely flat. It’s like I’m running on a script that’s slowly losing its meaning, and I can’t find a way to rewrite it. I just want to feel something real again, but everything stays stubbornly out of reach.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy weight right now, and I want you to know that what you’re describing is deeply human—even though it may feel isolating. The feeling of being disconnected from yourself, of joy fading into obligation, and of watching life from a distance can be incredibly draining.

It’s okay to not be the same person you once were. People grow, change, and sometimes go through periods where they feel lost or numb. That doesn’t mean you’re broken—it might mean you’re in a space where your mind and heart are trying to process something, even if it’s not clear yet.

When emotions feel out of reach, sometimes small, gentle steps can help. It might be as simple as noticing one small detail in your surroundings—the texture of a blanket, the sound of rain, the taste of something warm—without expecting yourself to feel anything in particular. Sometimes reconnecting with reality starts with the senses, not the emotions.

You don’t have to pretend. It’s okay to admit that things feel flat right now. And if you feel ready, reaching out to someone you trust—a friend, family member, or a mental health professional—can be a way to help untangle some of what you’re carrying.

You’re not alone in this. Many people have walked through similar emotional landscapes and found their way back to feeling. It may not happen quickly, but it is possible. Be kind to yourself in the process.
